title: Adam McKay on ‘Vice,’ Power, Comedy, and Criticism | Interview (Ep. 114)
format: Sean Fennessey talks to Adam McKay about Vice, the 50-year story behind Dick Cheney, the heart attack he had after the shoot, and why this is the least cool movie he has made.

films.title: Vice
films.note: The subject. McKay set out to make a portrait of a man, then found the first act was really Lynne Cheney's, and had to fold in the 50-year rise of the Republican right around him. He cut a musical number and a teenage-love run that tested badly, reordered the opening around the DUI and 9/11, and says Hank Corwin's cutaways (fly fishing, a lion taking prey) doubled in the edit. Sourced from Jane Mayer, Ron Suskind, and Barton Gellman. He had a heart attack after the shoot.

films.title: The Big Short
films.note: His pivot away from comedy, adapted from a book he loved after the script stalled at Plan B. He argues banks aren't polarizing the way Vice is, and that the financial collapse and the Republican revolution harmonize: deregulation let bank money buy off Congress.

films.title: Step Brothers
films.note: His touchstone for making peace with hostile reviews. He and Will Farrell agreed up front that a fart joke meant no good notices; he jokes it landed 50 percent good, 50 percent bad.

films.title: Anchorman: The Legend of Ron Burgundy
films.note: Where the 16mm short films Lorne Michaels let him direct at SNL, with cameos from Buscemi, Ben Stiller, and Willem Dafoe, eventually led.

films.title: The Man Who Would Be King
films.note: Named among his favorite movies as proof he'll watch anything.

films.title: Breaking Away
films.note: Another lifelong favorite he rattles off.

films.title: One on One
films.note: The Robby Benson movie, which he flags as a Bill Simmons favorite.

films.title: Airplane!
films.note: Cited as the far end of his taste, right next to the prestige films.

films.title: GoodFellas
films.note: His yardstick for whether Vice plays arch: he wonders if the Cheney-Rumsfeld no-bid-contract exchange reads like Paul Sorvino, or just like what they'd actually have said.

films.title: Idiocracy
films.note: One of his all-time favorite comedies, which he says streaming and cable rescued from being lost.

films.title: Office Space
films.note: Paired with Idiocracy as a comedy that only found its audience through cable and streaming.

films.title: You Were Never Really Here
films.note: His pick for the last great thing he's seen. He praises Lynne Ramsay's fractured, impressionistic style on a straightforward script, the Joaquin Phoenix two-hander, and, on a 12-year-old level, that the weapon is a hammer.

Ads: Ringer podcast network / Golden Globes livestream promo
